title: Advanced Problem-Solving Patterns (Capstone)
description: A recognition guide tying every technique from Two Pointers through Dynamic Programming together -- not new syntax, but the map for choosing the right tool when facing a new problem.
objectives: Recognize which pattern a new problem resembles, based on its shape and constraints
See how a single problem can sometimes be solved with more than one pattern
Build a mental checklist for approaching an unfamiliar DSA problem

sections.text: 1. Why This Lesson Has (Almost) No New Code

Every technique needed is already covered -- Two Pointers, Sliding Window, Prefix Sum, Hashing, Binary Search, Recursion, Backtracking, Greedy, and Dynamic Programming. The hardest real-world skill isn't knowing an algorithm exists -- it's recognizing WHICH one applies to a brand new problem you've never seen. This lesson is that recognition guide.

sections.title: Problem signals -> likely technique
rows: A SORTED array, and pairs/triples summing to a target
Two Pointers (from opposite ends)
A CONTIGUOUS subarray/substring, min/max/count over all windows
Sliding Window (fixed or variable size)
MANY range-sum queries on a fixed array
Prefix Sum
MANY range-update operations, read final result only
Difference Array
"Have I seen this before?" / counting occurrences / pair lookups
Hashing (HashMap/HashSet)
A SORTED array, searching for a value or boundary
Binary Search
Exploring ALL possible choices, with the ability to abandon invalid paths
Backtracking
Repeatedly making the 'best right now' choice, with a PROVABLE greedy property
Greedy
Overlapping sub-problems, and asking 'what's the best/count/way' up to some state
Dynamic Programming (memoization or tabulation)
Tree/graph structure, visiting nodes level by level
BFS
Tree/graph structure, diving deep down one path first
DFS
Checking bits directly, or a single unpaired/unique value among duplicates
Bit Manipulation

sections.text: 3. Worked Example: Recognizing Multiple Valid Approaches
Take the pair-sum problem: given an array and a target, does any pair sum to it? We've now seen THREE different ways to approach variants of this exact question across this course.

sections.title: Choosing between them
points: If the array is ALREADY sorted (or sorting it is acceptable/cheap), Two Pointers uses less extra memory
If the array is UNSORTED and sorting isn't desired, Hashing solves it in one pass without reordering anything
Both beat brute force's O(n^2) -- recognizing this is more valuable than memorizing one 'correct' answer

sections.text: 4. Worked Example: A Problem That Combines Patterns

Some real problems blend multiple techniques. Consider: 'find the length of the longest substring with all unique characters.' This is fundamentally a SLIDING WINDOW problem (contiguous substring, window grows/shrinks) COMBINED with HASHING (a Set tracking characters currently in the window).

This is Sliding Window's variable-size expand/shrink structure, using a HashSet (Hashing) to check membership in O(1) instead of scanning the current window every time. Recognizing that TWO patterns combine here -- rather than treating this as some entirely new, unfamiliar problem -- is exactly the skill this capstone lesson is building.
5. A Practical Approach to a New Problem

sections.title: A mental checklist when facing an unfamiliar problem
points: What's the input shape? (array, string, tree, graph, sorted or not?)
What's actually being asked? (existence, count, minimum/maximum, all possibilities?)
Does brute force work, and what's ITS complexity? (always a valid, if slow, starting point)
Does the problem's shape match any pattern from the table above?
Could two patterns combine, like Sliding Window + Hashing here?
If nothing fits cleanly, can Dynamic Programming decompose it into overlapping sub-problems?

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to memorize solutions to specific problems instead of recognizing the underlying PATTERN -- patterns transfer to new problems, memorized solutions don't.
Assuming every problem has exactly ONE correct approach -- many have multiple valid solutions with different trade-offs (like pair-sum's Two Pointers vs Hashing).
Jumping straight to a complex technique (DP, backtracking) before checking if a simpler pattern (two pointers, sliding window, hashing) already solves it more directly.
Not writing the brute force solution first, even mentally -- it's often the fastest way to understand the problem correctly before optimizing.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: When solving an unfamiliar DSA problem, what should you check before choosing an approach?
questions.answer: The input's shape (sorted array, string, tree, graph?), what's actually being asked (existence, count, min/max, all possibilities?), whether a brute-force approach works as a starting baseline, and whether the problem's shape matches a known pattern -- possibly more than one combined.

questions.question: Give an example of a problem that can be solved with more than one valid pattern.
questions.answer: Pair-sum: Two Pointers works in O(n) time and O(1) space if the array is sorted; Hashing works in O(n) time and O(n) space on an unsorted array without needing to sort first -- both are valid, with different trade-offs.

questions.question: How can Sliding Window and Hashing combine to solve a single problem?
questions.answer: In problems like 'longest substring with all unique characters,' a sliding window expands and shrinks over a contiguous range, while a HashSet tracks which characters are currently inside the window, giving O(1) membership checks instead of rescanning the window each time.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: The real skill in DSA problem-solving is pattern recognition, not memorization: matching a new problem's shape (sorted array? contiguous range? tree/graph? overlapping sub-problems?) to a known technique from Two Pointers through Dynamic Programming. Many problems have multiple valid approaches with different trade-offs -- pair-sum solves via Two Pointers (sorted, O(1) space) or Hashing (unsorted, O(n) space) -- and some problems combine two patterns at once, like Sliding Window plus Hashing for longest-unique-substring. A practical approach: identify the input shape and the actual question being asked, start from brute force as a baseline, then look for a matching (or combined) pattern to optimize.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Don't memorize ANSWERS -- memorize the SHAPE each pattern solves. Sorted + pair-sum = Two Pointers. Contiguous + window = Sliding Window. 'Have I seen this?' = Hashing. Overlapping sub-problems = DP. Match the shape, not the specific problem.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What questions should you ask yourself when facing an unfamiliar DSA problem?
Name two different valid patterns for solving pair-sum, and their trade-offs.
How do Sliding Window and Hashing combine in the longest-unique-substring problem?
Why is pattern recognition more valuable than memorizing individual solutions?

sections.title: Maximum Subarray
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array, find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum and return that sum.
hints: Kadane's algorithm: at each position, decide whether to extend the current running subarray or start fresh from this element -- whichever gives a larger sum.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Kadane's algorithm: track current running sum, resetting to just the current element whenever the running sum would drop below it (i.e. the prior run was net-negative), tracking the best sum seen.

sections.title: Product of Array Except Self
sections.problemStatement: Given an array, return an array where each element is the product of all OTHER elements, without using division and in O(n) time.
hints: result[i] = (product of everything to the left of i) * (product of everything to the right of i).
Compute left-products in one pass, then multiply in right-products in a second pass -- no extra array needed for the right side if done in-place.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: First pass left to right fills result[i] with the product of all elements to i's left. Second pass right to left multiplies in a running right-product, avoiding division entirely and using only O(1) extra space beyond the output.

sections.title: Subarray Sum Equals K
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array and an integer k, return the number of contiguous subarrays whose sum equals k.
hints: A subarray [i+1..j] sums to k exactly when prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i] == k.
Keep a hashmap of prefixSum -> count of times seen; at each j, look up prefixSum[j] - k to count how many earlier prefixes would give a valid subarray.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Track running prefix sum and a hashmap of prefix-sum frequencies (seeded with {0: 1} for subarrays starting at index 0). At each step, add the count of (currentSum - k) seen so far to the answer, then record the current sum.

sections.title: Find the Duplicate Number
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of n+1 integers where each is in [1, n], exactly one value repeats (possibly more than once). Find it without modifying the array and using O(1) extra space.
hints: Treat values as pointers: index i points to nums[i]. Because a duplicate exists, following these pointers repeatedly must enter a cycle.
This becomes Floyd's cycle detection (same as Linked List Cycle II) -- find the meeting point, then find the cycle's entrance, which is the duplicate value.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Floyd's Tortoise and Hare on the implicit 'linked list' formed by treating nums[i] as a pointer to index nums[i]. Phase 1 finds a meeting point inside the cycle. Phase 2 resets one pointer to the start; both now move one step at a time and meet exactly at the cycle's entrance, which is the duplicate.

sections.title: First Missing Positive
sections.problemStatement: Given an unsorted integer array, find the smallest missing positive integer, in O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
hints: The answer must be in range [1, n+1], so values outside that range (negatives, zero, or too large) can be ignored.
Use the array ITSELF as a hash set: place each value v (1 <= v <= n) at index v-1 by swapping, then scan for the first index i where nums[i] != i+1 -- that gap is the answer.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Cyclic placement: for each index, while its value is a valid positive in range [1, n] and not already correctly placed, swap it to its 'home' index (value v goes to index v-1). Afterward, the first index where nums[i] != i+1 reveals the missing positive; if all match, the answer is n+1.

sections.title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
sections.problemStatement: Given two sorted arrays, return the median of the combined data, in O(log(m+n)) time.

examples.explanation: Merged sorted: [1,2,3], median is the middle value 2.

hints: Merging both arrays is O(m+n), too slow for the required O(log(m+n)).

Binary search on the SMALLER array for a partition point such that all elements on the left of both partitions are <= all elements on the right -- the median can be read directly from the four border values around that partition.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Merge both arrays (like merge step of merge sort) into one sorted array, then read off the middle element(s). Correct but O(m+n), not the required O(log(m+n)).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search a partition index on the smaller array. For a candidate partition, the corresponding partition on the other array is determined by the combined-left-half size. Adjust the binary search until the max of the left halves <= the min of the right halves; the median is derived directly from those four boundary values.

sections.title: Minimum Window Substring
sections.problemStatement: Given strings s and t, return the smallest substring of s that contains every character of t (including duplicates), or "" if none exists.
hints: Variable-size sliding window: expand the right edge until the window contains all of t's characters, then shrink from the left as much as possible while still valid, recording the smallest valid window along the way.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Track required character counts from t and a 'have' counter of how many distinct required characters are currently fully satisfied in the window. Expand right; whenever all requirements are satisfied, shrink left greedily, updating the best window, until a requirement is violated.

sections.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of bar heights forming a histogram (each bar width 1), return the area of the largest rectangle that fits entirely within the histogram.

examples.explanation: Bars at indices 2-3 (heights 5, 6) give rectangle of height 5 spanning width 2 = 10.

hints: Monotonic increasing stack of indices: when a shorter bar is encountered, it means the bars on the stack taller than it can't extend any further right -- pop and compute their rectangle area using the current index as the right boundary.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Maintain a monotonic increasing stack of bar indices. When the current bar is shorter than the stack's top, pop and compute the popped bar's rectangle area (height = popped bar, width = current index - new stack top index - 1), tracking the max. A sentinel 0-height bar appended at the end forces all remaining bars to be resolved.

sections.title: Sliding Window Maximum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array and a window size k, return the maximum value in each window as it slides from the start to the end of the array.
hints: Maintain a monotonic DECREASING deque of indices -- the front always holds the current window's max index.
Before adding a new index, pop smaller values from the back (they can never be the max while the new, later, bigger value is in the window). Pop from the front when it slides out of the window range.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Monotonic decreasing deque of indices. For each new index: remove back indices whose values are smaller than the current (they're dominated), remove the front index if it's now outside the window, push the current index, and once the window is full record the front's value as that window's max.
